speaker: interviewer
text: An interview with Dr. Michael Okunieff. Can you describe your role in the liberation of Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. They gave us a order from Moscow to leave the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], to leave the partisan area and to go on to block the road, Vilna [NON-ENGLISH] and from-- and the surrounding and to prevent the retreat of German army. All of the partisan-- about five-- probably about 3,000 to 4,000 were collected. And we went-- went to Vilna on the road. We didn't see, in the beginning, too many German. We saw a lot of tracks. Our group was practically one of the first ones. We-- we approach Vilna, just on the-- on the outskirt. And at that time, we united-- united with the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we united, and we had a lot of shooting over there. The Russian army didn't wanted to go in first in Vilna. So they think [? you should ?] send us because we were familiar with the-- with area. [NON-ENGLISH]-- this mean as-- to find out what is going on. What's the other word? [NON-ENGLISH] is the-- is the people what are going on front-- Reconnaissance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reconnaissance. OK. We went in as a reconnaissance. And the Germans started to shoot on us. We shoot them. We-- we discovered all the point where they were shooting. Before we know this, we had the already Russian airplanes putting up some-- some bombs. And we were going step-- house by house to clean it up. And I was one of the first one, practically, to-- to liberate the city. We met over there the head of the-- the-- their White Russian Army, General Chernyakhovsky. And we met some of the high dignitaries. Before we know this, my-- my aunt was a professor of Moscow University. And she and Professor Ribielski had set out to cover the hospital in-- on [NON-ENGLISH]. And she induced me to start-- to go back to school, to the university. And [INAUDIBLE] there. But soon after, I left. And on the way to Vilna, I-- I-- to-- to liberate Vilna, we met over there. If [? we ?] [? would ?] [INAUDIBLE], but we found in the ruin a German officer. And we were three-- three partisan. We took him in. We started to-- to-- to interrogate him-- interrogate him. And my friends saw him. Kill him. Kill him. We-- we don't have to bring him any. And he started to begging here. He was probably 20, 21. He was begging-- begging to forgive his life. And here, he's-- he's innocent. And he was begging over there. Finally, the-- the-- the heart was so-- I was so angry. And you see, I came in. I felt so-- so let down. I lost the family. I lost everything. I saw before my eyes almost fire. I couldn't sleep. I saw-- I saw how they-- they destroyed the kids in Lida. I saw the beatings that-- that continued, [? see, ?] for-- for already three years what I did not-- what continuously barrage of emotional destruction. And I felt in here that-- almost that, emotionally, I wanted to release.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- so I wented to-- to the-- my friends and said, why don't you kill him? And they were screaming to me, kill. And I said, why don't you kill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I did not-- I could not bring myself to kill. And this guy started to beg-- I have a family. Just leave me in here. So I said, OK, we'll bring him to commandeur-- to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Commanditor. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Commanditor, ?] to the headquarters. We brought him in over there. And sure enough, they took out-- they take a look on-- on his arm. He was a Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: An SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: SS. I regretted this stuff all my life. In here that we-- we didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't kill him, right. Now, but in a way, from the other side, I don't-- I didn't have a heart to-- to-- to kill a person when-- when is he-- when he's disarmed. Now, the thing is, I'm running away from Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, for a while, but you-- you mentioned then your aunt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, aunt-- I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: --got you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, got me on the University of Vilna. And I'm starting to study. And I'm joining the faculty of-- of medicine. And I-- after that, it's starting-- the hunger and-- and the-- and the emotional-- emotional pressure from-- from being in Vilna was too much. And [? I'm ?] going away to-- to Lvov, over there. And I had to go to the army. I didn't take-- I did not just go into the army right away. This was a problem. They could even call me a deserter. And other there, I'm going with the-- with the Russian army and in-- in the wagon. And suddenly, I saw that they will put me to-- a officer comes over to me. And I thought that-- that this is my end. They will probably send me to the-- through the-- [NON-ENGLISH], to the concentration in [NON-ENGLISH]. But instead of it, he was Jewish. He said-- I told him, I'm-- I'm a partisan, that before-- not as-- before-- before I turned around, he said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm a-- this is a hero to-- to all these Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was on the way to Lvov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lvov. And he's let me-- they let me-- I'm-- I was a celebrity. From there-- and from Lvov, I went to Poland. And in Poland, in here-- Poland, after the war, was so anti-Semitic. There are still so much hate, so much that survivor that did not-- first of all, they wanted to-- to clean their hands. They-- the Christian world had a feeling had on their hands, the blood. They wanted to wipe the blood off their hands and to wipe their-- their mischief or-- or whatever--

speaker: interviewer
text: Guilt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guilt. And this is a-- and we were-- we were practically the sign what they did. So they wanted just to wipe out from the surface of the Earth their-- their guilt. And-- and it started in here, such-- such anti-Semitism. Horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the beginning, I'm-- I'm landing, I believe, Lodz, if I'm not mistaken. And [? all was ?] there-- I-- we have a kibbutz. I became part of the kibbutz. I was more intelligent, maybe, from the rest of them. I was teaching them. And already, I was a university student. So [? and I was ?] there. But there was no future to send us in-- to send us to Katowice. They killed, over there, some-- a lot of-- of young kids. And the-- we joined the group. And then being a partisan, they gave me arms, and we're protected.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from other there, they send me to Czestochowa. And they send me-- I'm sorry-- they send me to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- to the border of Karpaty. And they-- and they told me in here, you are partisan. We need somebody for Bricha. We had some problem. They killed a couple of kids on-- on the road of the Karpaty. Karpaty is the highest mountain in Poland. And we have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: --take you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was sending you? Who was giving you orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was Shlomo Brand. Shlomo Brand was the head over there of-- from our-- he was one of the commandeers from our [? truck. ?] Abba Kovner was in the group, too. But Abba Kovner was already on the way. He was, I believe, in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was still from the-- this was a-- a partisan commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a partisan commander. But it was from-- for [INAUDIBLE]. It was-- it was practically, from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: part-- part of Bricha?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Bricha, yeah. This was a Bricha.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Bricha.

speaker: interviewee
text: But whom could they use? They could use only our guys because, first of all, we have the preparation. We had-- we have the experience. And we had-- we had the way of doing things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went in, and he told me-- he give me a gun. And he said, three days ago, they killed three-- three people in here. It's very dangerous in here. And there were-- I had about 50 kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a provodnik. Provodnik-- this mean a-- a guy what was a leader. The-- the guy what-- what showed us how to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: A guide.

speaker: interviewee
text: A guide, guide. Yeah, and we had a guide. And with the guide-- I did not know, by myself, how to go through-- through the high mountains. This is the highest mountains over there, almost on the Eastern Europe. And after we left with him, while we were going with-- while we were going with him, suddenly, they started, high on the mountain, in here. And it was muddy. And they were so tired. And there was the kids. I could not believe it, how they could do it. I could not do it, what I-- for-- for days, and weeks, and months, I was going every single day. We were making-- we were going for miles and miles. And they were following like-- like a clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were alone with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was maybe a couple-- a couple of others.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But most of them-- I-- I was in here. But they were 13, 14. And in here, I would like to describe one of the guys, And there was a kid of 13 years. His leg was swollen. He was-- he was torn up. No shoes. In-- in-- in-- it was snow and-- and-- and mud. And there was-- and they were shooting in here. And how this kid was following me, I could not believe it. Finally, we made it through. And the thing is, I thought they would kill us because the Polacks was-- the-- the-- the-- they were almost on our heads. The-- the bullets were-- were almost going-- we were laying down. And we could almost feel the bullets flying over our heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we came to the-- we came a little farther. The guide disappears. I'm by myself. What to do? I don't know the-- I didn't know the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, after years already in the woods, in partisan, you get a, like, intuition, a feeling how to go. Anyway, I took them out through the mountains. I went on the other side. Finally, we saw the Czech border. [? As soon ?] [? as it ?] [? was ?] Czech border, we went-- we went down. I took the-- the gun. And I-- I, over there, hide it because they told me that the Czech, if they find arms, they can-- they'll put you in jail. And from over there, I went to Prague. And from Prague, I went to Germany. And I-- I continued with my study.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Palestine at that time? Why didn't you go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I started my study. And I was in halfway in my-- in my-- in my study. As was so much-- I had so much-- such a desire to finish up the schooling that and in here, I had occasion. The groups-- there was a group over there of students. It was some friends from Vilna. And all my three-- in Vilna, what remained-- from all the Vilna, we had only four students on-- on the university was remain. And all the four students were-- and all the three, in addition to me, were already in-- in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich. So I joined them where we'll finish altogether. One is in here. One is New York. And one is in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, they didn't wanted to let me in. Why they didn't want to? They had a feeling that I am not kosher. I did not want-- they wanted to know why-- what I did in-- after '43. And to tell that why I was in partisan-- this mean I am a Soviet spy. So they mad at me. So I had two years, till '51, what I was-- what I was in-- in-- in Munich, in Munich. And I was taking some psychology and all that after-- after I graduated. And from over there, I made my doctor. And from there-- from as a doctor, I went to New York in '51. And I specialized in the-- in internal medicine, and after that, anesthesia. And in '56, I got married. And we have four children. And one is in Israel. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many-- how many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have nine-- nine grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the nine grandchildren are all-- are all in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: All in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you established your-- a-- a medical practice here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you became attached to a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a-- I am-- I was delegate to Chicago Medical Society. I was a delegate-- delegate to Illinois Medical Society. I'm Who Is Who in America. I'm Who Is Who in Midwest. I'm Who Is Who in international in-- in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in internal medicine? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --family practice. In family practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: In family practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Family practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote quite a bit of work. And I was famous. I introduced some-- I was helping to shape the Chicago Medical Society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come to Chicago? What brought you to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing is, when I came in to-- to New York, I had to wait about a year or two before I could nostrificate myself. Besides, I want to get my license. In here, I flew out without knowing even the language. After two months, I took my examination. I passed. I don't know how I did it. But I passed. And I-- by-- by passing in here, I already was a licensed physician. So I said, I don't wanted to-- to wait that long. And I went in here to-- at the University of Illinois. I make my residency. And I remained in here. And [? that was ?] [? story. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Thank you. We are-- we are now going to go to the portion of the interview where we show some pictures and introduce your wife. But in that portion, I also want to ask you how your experiences in the Holocaust shaped your-- your life and your thinking. So we'll stop here, and then we'll go to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a-- this is a picture from my father and my older brother, [? Beryl. ?] My-- the name of my father was Pinchas. And my-- my son-- my oldest son has his name. And this-- the other one-- the older brother was Gershon. And I give to my older-- to my son both names-- Paul Gershon. My father died in 1941. He was caught by the German with my brother, while they were trying to save what we had. And brought to-- and they were killed at Ponary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the-- this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture I got from my aunt, what was living in-- in Russia-- in Soviet Union. And she came in later to-- to Israel. And she send it to me. This is a picture of my mother. My mother was a finished with a gold medal in-- in Russia. She was intelligent woman. And was always the intellectual type. And we had a family. Our family was three boys and-- and mother, father. And this-- this picture is me, Michael Okunieff. This is in the first days after we liberated Vilna. At that time, in here, I was part of the group what helped to control and to-- and to bring order to Vilna. This is the way I was-- I looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- who took this picture? And how did you-- and you kept it with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they took-- they took my picture, I believe, from the partisans. And I had the picture with me. And I brought this when I was going-- I-- one time, I left Vilna to-- to Minsk. And I was there, I left the picture. And this is a picture what I got it. This is a picture from Schlemke [? Salomon. ?] He's in-- he is in Vilna now. He was at Naroch. He was-- he was in Naroch first in the partisan. He was-- he spent most of the time in the ghetto and surrounding with me. And he was-- he was wounded during-- later, with-- in a fight with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my aunt. She was a professor of psychiatry in Moscow University. And she was killed by Stalin during the persecutions. OK. OK. Her name is Okuniewa. She was very famous in Russia. This is me. This is a picture from-- from my cousin. And my-- my aunt what survived-- survived Hitler. The aunt is [? Rachel. ?] She was a pharmacist. And my-- my cousin was-- oh, forgotten here. OK. We'll make a--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mayer. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mayer. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mayer. ?] OK. This is my wedding picture. And this is my wife, Beverly Kailes. And this is Michael Okunieff. And we got married in 1956-- in 1956, about 40 years ago. OK. This is a picture of my cousin, a doctor. And she with her husband. And she was very famous in the partisans. She-- she brought out quite a few groups of-- of partisans to the woods. Her father was in the-- in the area. And this is the reason why she knew the area-- knew the-- the terrain. OK. This is my son, Paul, with his wife, Debra. He's the head of Cancer Institute of America. This is my oldest daughter, [? Paula. ?] She lives in Boston. And she is the engineer. This is my second oldest-- the third oldest. And her name is [? Lisa. ?] She likes her name Rina-- to be called Rina. She's marry-- she's a Habbanic. She's married to a Habbanic. She has nine children, and she lives in Israel in Kfar Chabad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim. This is my youngest, Rhoda. She lives in New York. She is a statistician. She works for-- for insurance company. This is my daughter, Rina, with her husband, Chaim, with-- she has nine children. But only six are in the picture. This was taken in Kfar Chabad. This is [NON-ENGLISH] Shneur, my oldest grandson. Two, four, six-- this is the rest of the children, except for the infant. And this-- the picture was taken to-- for the occasion of Shneur's bar mitzvah. This is my wife, Beverly, of 40 years. We have four children and nine grandchildren. And we are-- I am a physician. She's a housewife. And-- and I love her. [LAUGHTER] And we have a nice-- a nice life together for the last 40 years. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, as far as your Holocaust experiences-- is there a message? Is there something that comes from them that you would like to relate, that you would like to tell the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: If any catastrophe bring-- bring with you a certain change of ideas and some lessons what you want to bring to the world. First of all, I would not like to see the world to have a catastrophe like we had before. I think we have to improve, and to teach, and to educate the people of the sequence of hate, and if there-- and try to maybe to prevent it. And I think prevention is probably best achieved by a certain amount of force. You have to prepare yourself. And when you see that you have-- that the evil takes over the good, maybe to find a way to just squinch them-- to-- to destroy the evil before it become powerful. The second thing is evil should be fight with evil. And the-- in-- in a world where you feel that you are the victim, arm yourself. Protect yourself. And be-- and try to be strong, and face the challenge. A-- in the Jewish religion, told us that war is evil. It's against God. But they did not tell us how to-- how to fight it. The Christian world glorify, in a way, the-- the wars in history. Maybe we should go halfway-- to-- not to glorify, but at least to learn how to protect ourselves. The second thing is in-- in time of disaster, to learn to unite ourself and to be able to fight back the enemy or the-- the sinister forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that your Holocaust experience has changed you, personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: After you go through such a tremendous emotional trauma, you start-- you start to appreciate life. You started even to love life more than before. And you started-- you-- you started believe in some force, what we may call God. Why? In order-- because without it, there is tyranny, destruction, and-- and there is no outlet for our emotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Thanks very much for sharing all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. This is the last kiss that I give you. [LAUGHS] [LAUGHTER]
